assessedLearningOutcomes: Students can define chemical substances and their properties.
Students can classify elements as metals or non-metals based on oxide formation.
Students can distinguish between acidic, basic, and neutral oxides.
Students can predict the nature of an oxide formed by a given element.
assessedConcepts: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Nature of Oxides
conceptCoveragePriorities: Classification of oxides based on element type
Predicting oxide nature from element properties
misconceptionTargets: Confusing physical change with chemical change in combustion

sectionPlan.title: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
sectionPlan.focus: Session-aligned assessment on chemical substances and oxide properties

conceptDistribution.concept: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Nature of Oxides

learningOutcomeCoverage.outcome: Students can define chemical substances and their properties.
Students can classify elements as metals or non-metals based on oxide formation.
Students can distinguish between acidic, basic, and neutral oxides.
Students can predict the nature of an oxide formed by a given element.

timeAllocation.section: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour

instructions: Answer all questions using only the concepts taught in this session.
For MCQs, select the most appropriate option.
For short answers, provide concise and accurate scientific reasoning.

sections.title: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
questions.prompt: Which of the following elements reacts with oxygen to form a basic oxide?
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can classify elements as metals or non-metals based on oxide formation.
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: Metals react with oxygen to form basic oxides (e.g., MgO).

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
questions.prompt: When Carbon burns in air, it forms Carbon Dioxide (CO2). What is the nature of this oxide?
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can distinguish between acidic, basic, and neutral oxides.
conceptRefs: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Nature of Oxides
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: Non-metals react with oxygen to form acidic or neutral oxides (e.g., CO2).

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
questions.prompt: Which litmus paper would turn red when dipped in a solution of Sulfur Dioxide (SO2) dissolved in water?
options: C. Both blue and red litmus paper
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can distinguish between acidic, basic, and neutral oxides.
conceptRefs: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Nature of Oxides
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: Non-metal oxides are generally acidic, turning blue litmus red.

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can define chemical substances and their properties.
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: Chemical substances are forms of matter that have a constant composition and distinct properties.

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
questions.prompt: Name one metal that reacts violently with oxygen to form a basic oxide.
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can classify elements as metals or non-metals based on oxide formation.
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: Sodium reacts violently with oxygen.

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
questions.prompt: Explain why Magnesium Oxide (MgO) turns red litmus paper blue.
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can distinguish between acidic, basic, and neutral oxides.
conceptRefs: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Nature of Oxides
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: MgO is a basic oxide; basic oxides turn red litmus blue.

questions.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
questions.prompt: Predict the nature of the oxide formed when Iron burns in air. Justify your answer.
questions.expectedLength: Prediction and justification based on element type.
learningOutcomeRefs: Students can predict the nature of an oxide formed by a given element.
sourceEvidence: Teacher notes: Metals generally form basic oxides; Iron is a metal.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.explanation: Magnesium is a metal, and metals react with oxygen to form basic oxides.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.explanation: Carbon is a non-metal, and non-metal oxides like CO2 are acidic.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.explanation: Sulfur Dioxide forms an acidic solution in water, which turns blue litmus red.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.answer: A chemical substance is a form of matter that has a constant composition and distinct properties.
items.explanation: Definition based on the session overview.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.explanation: Sodium is known to react violently with oxygen.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.answer: Magnesium Oxide is a basic oxide. Basic oxides react with water to form bases, which turn red litmus paper blue.
items.explanation: Explanation of the chemical behavior of MgO as a basic oxide.

items.sectionTitle: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
items.answer: The oxide formed will be basic. Iron is a metal, and metals react with oxygen to form basic oxides.
items.explanation: Justification based on the classification of Iron as a metal.

awardGuidance: Award full marks only for the correct option.
Award full marks only for the correct option.
Award full marks only for the correct option.
Award marks for mentioning constant composition and distinct properties.
Award marks for naming a metal known to react violently.

markBreakdown.criterion: Explains reaction with litmus (turns red to blue)

awardGuidance: Award 1 mark for identifying the nature, 1 mark for the explanation.

markBreakdown.criterion: Justifies using Iron as a metal

awardGuidance: Award 1 mark for prediction, 1 mark for correct justification.

criteria.criterion: First valid point: Identifies MgO as basic oxide
Second valid point: Explains the litmus color change correctly

items.sampleAnswer: Magnesium Oxide is a basic oxide. Basic oxides turn red litmus paper blue.

criteria.criterion: First valid point: Correctly predicts the oxide is basic
Second valid point: Justifies that Iron is a metal which forms basic oxides

items.sampleAnswer: The oxide will be basic because Iron is a metal, and metals form basic oxides.

criteria.criterion: First valid point: Identifies MgO as basic oxide
Second valid point: Explains the litmus color change correctly

items.sampleAnswer: Magnesium Oxide is a basic oxide. Basic oxides turn red litmus paper blue.

criteria.criterion: First valid point: Correctly predicts the oxide is basic
Second valid point: Justifies that Iron is a metal which forms basic oxides

items.sampleAnswer: The oxide will be basic because Iron is a metal, and metals form basic oxides.

generalInstructions: Award marks point-wise for each valid idea in short answer questions.
For MCQs, only the correct option receives marks.
evaluatorInstructions: Use only the taught session content when evaluating student responses.
Ensure scientific terminology (e.g., 'basic oxide', 'acidic') is used correctly.
moderationNotes: Maintain consistency across equivalent answers for short answer questions.

coverageSummary: Balanced coverage of chemical substances, oxide classification, and litmus testing concepts.
balanceNotes: Paper progresses from easier recall questions to application and analysis tasks.

details: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour: 7 question(s), 9 mark(s)
No untaught or future-session content included.
